comments: This manuscript is a position paper arguing that semantic-web research should pay more attention to the problem of reference and grounding, and that "reference systems" grounded in human perceptual and social operations (e.g., geodetic systems, measurement scales, calendars, conceptual spaces) can provide a bottom-up semantic integration layer complementary to ontologies. Strengths: the topic is timely and relevant to the semantic-web community; the paper connects work on symbol grounding, reference, conceptual spaces and geospatial examples in a coherent high-level argument; it emphasizes the human-centric operational perspective which is a useful corrective to purely syntax- and ontology-driven approaches. Weaknesses: the provided text contains many repetitions and formatting/extraction artifacts that make reading difficult; the contribution is predominately conceptual and lacks concrete proposals, formalization, algorithms, or empirical evaluation in this version (the paper refers to prior work but does not present new case studies or results here); comparisons to alternative approaches (URIs, formal semantics, existing linked-data practices) are at a high level and could be deeper and more critical; it is unclear how the suggested "new kinds of reference systems" would be specified, implemented, and evaluated in practice or integrated with current semantic-web stacks. Recommendations for improvement: significantly clean up and reorganize the manuscript to remove duplications and improve flow; state clearly the novel contribution of this paper versus the authors' prior work; include at least one concrete worked example or design pattern showing how an observation-based reference system can be encoded and used with Linked Data (or outline a clear implementation/experimental plan); deepen the discussion of related work and trade-offs with existing technical approaches; discuss evaluation criteria and possible experiments to demonstrate utility. Overall assessment: an interesting and useful position paper that would benefit from clearer presentation and more concrete material to increase its technical substance and practical impact.
